humanities_role_explanation: Includes humanities subjects such as Classics, Philosophy, Musicology, Art History, Archaeology, and Literature as part of its multi-disciplinary evaluation of expert-level general knowledge.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: A multimodal benchmark where 86% of the questions are text-only and approximately 14% combine text with an image reference.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates multi-step expert reasoning, factual recognition of highly specialized academic concepts, translation of ancient scripts, and domain-specific symbolic reasoning (e.g., in musicology or formal logic).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed as a broad academic benchmark spanning dozens of subjects across STEM, humanities, and social sciences.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates frontier LLMs such as GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking, o1, o3-mini, DeepSeek-R1, and Grok 2.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems under evaluation to measure the gap between current AI capabilities and human expertise.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes questions in Classics, Musicology, Philosophy, Art History, Poetry (Literature), and Archaeology.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates models using Accuracy (%) and RMS Calibration Error (%).

published_after_march_2023.note: Published as a preprint/paper in early 2026.

english_language.note: The paper and the benchmark questions are written in English.

summary: This paper introduces Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), a multi-modal benchmark consisting of 2,500 highly challenging, expert-level questions across dozens of academic subjects. The questions are crowdsourced from global subject-matter experts and vetted through a rigorous pipeline to ensure they cannot be easily retrieved online and are currently too difficult for frontier LLMs to solve. The benchmark is designed to serve as a high-difficulty standard for tracking AI capabilities as existing academic benchmarks reach saturation.
key_findings: State-of-the-art LLMs demonstrate very low accuracy on HLE, with the best-performing reasoning model (o3-mini high) achieving only 13.4% accuracy on the text-only subset, while non-reasoning models like GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet score between 2.7% and 4.1% overall. Furthermore, all evaluated models exhibit poor calibration with RMS calibration errors exceeding 70%, indicating a tendency to output incorrect answers with high confidence.
